policy_statement: As adaptation measures for increased short-time heavy rain, Hiroshima City will implement city renovation for disaster prevention, construct a disaster information sharing system, and enhance disaster information delivery systems.
mechanism_description: The city will renovate urban areas for disaster prevention and build/upgrade disaster information sharing and delivery systems to reduce risks and impacts from short-duration heavy rain.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall) → ↓ exposure/vulnerability via disaster-preventive urban design + improved warning/response information → ↓ flood impacts (risk, damages, casualties)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed system: Municipal governance and urban management—retrofitting/renovating urban areas for disaster prevention (e.g., drainage upgrades, flood-proofing, safer siting) and building/upgrading disaster information sharing and delivery systems (monitoring, alerts, communication channels, coordination platforms).  
(2) Mediating process: Improved stormwater conveyance/retention and reduced physical exposure, plus faster detection, warning dissemination, and coordinated emergency response that increases protective actions and reduces time-to-response.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower expected impacts from short-duration heavy rain and associated flooding (reduced risk, damages, service disruption, and potential loss of life) under a changing climate.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced harm (damage, disruption, casualties) from heavy-rain flooding by decreasing exposure/vulnerability through disaster-preventive urban renovation and improved warning/information delivery.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on flood risk by reducing exposure and vulnerability through physical urban renovation and improved disaster information/warning and coordination.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: The primary intended and largest climate effect is impact reduction from climate hazards (heavy rain/flooding), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), not primarily reducing resource/energy inputs (Resource Efficiency), and not centered on ecosystem processes (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some urban renovations (e.g., green infrastructure) could also create co-benefits like carbon sequestration or cooling, but the mechanism as specified is disaster prevention + information systems aimed at flood impact reduction, so Adaptation remains dominant.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism—disaster-preventive urban renovation plus disaster information sharing/delivery to address heavy rain—fundamentally operates by lowering flood impacts, which is adaptation by definition.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further categorization (e.g., “early warning systems” vs “flood-proof infrastructure”) requires policy-specific details and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “urban renovation for disaster prevention” is implemented mainly through nature-based measures (e.g., wetland restoration, large-scale green-blue networks), a Nature-Based Solutions label could be arguable; however, for this canonical mechanism emphasizing disaster prevention renovation + information systems, the dominant effect is adaptation via reduced flood exposure/vulnerability.
key_indicators: “adaptation measures for increased short-time heavy rain”; “City renovation for disaster prevention”; “construction of disaster information sharing system”; “enhancement of disaster information delivery system”; “Measures against increase of heavy rain in short time”
co_benefits: Improved general disaster preparedness and emergency communications for non-climate hazards; potential everyday public-safety/service reliability improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an information-system component that could also fit “planning-strategy” or “information-disclosure,” but the dominant action is implementation/build-out (construction/enhancement), so classified as infrastructure-investment.
